Common Themes And Application:
- Theme: The greatness and faithfulness of God
- Life application: Trusting in God's promises and seeking His guidance in all circumstances.
Symbols1Summarize: The most important symbol in these verses that recurs throughout the Bible is the "undivided heart." In this passage, the psalmist asks God for an undivided heart, representing a deep desire for wholehearted devotion to God. Throughout the Bible, an undivided heart is seen as the key to true worship and relationship with God, and it develops as a theme in various passages, such as in Jeremiah 32:39-40 and Matthew 22:37.
Symbols2Summarize: The most important symbol in these verses is the idol, representing false gods and the worship of man-made objects. This symbol recurs throughout the Bible, demonstrating the folly of idolatry and the need to worship the one true God. Examples of this can be found in Exodus 20:4-5, where God commands against idols, and in 1 Corinthians 8:4-6, where Paul emphasizes the importance of not worshiping idols. Overall, the idol symbolizes the dangers of worshiping anything other than God.
Symbols3Summarize: The most important symbol in these verses is the anchor. In the Bible, the anchor symbolizes hope and security. It represents the assurance of God's promises and the steadfastness of faith. This symbol develops throughout the Bible, from the story of Noah and the ark, where the ark served as an anchor during the flood, to the teachings of Jesus, who encouraged his followers to place their hope and trust in God's promises. The anchor symbolizes the unchanging nature of God's purpose and the certainty of salvation in Christ.
Summarize1: The psalmist asks God to teach him His ways and give him an undivided heart. He praises God for His love and deliverance, but also asks for mercy and strength against enemies. The psalmist seeks a sign of God's goodness to shame his enemies.
Summarize2: This passage from Isaiah criticizes those who make idols and worship them. It describes the process of crafting idols and emphasizes that they are created by human hands and have no power. The passage highlights the foolishness of idol worship and the shame that comes from it.
Summarize3: In Hebrews 6:13-20, it is mentioned that when God promised Abraham, He swore by Himself since there was no one greater. God wanted to make it clear that His purpose was unchanging, so He confirmed it with an oath. This gives us hope and serves as an anchor for our souls. Jesus, our forerunner, has entered the inner sanctuary on our behalf and has become a high priest forever.
Behindinsights1: One interesting insight not obvious in this passage is that this is a prayer of David. David was known for his deep reliance on and trust in God, even in the midst of adversity. This psalm reveals his faith in God's faithfulness, love, and protection. Additionally, it provides a glimpse into the challenges David faced and his plea for God's deliverance.
Behindinsights2: An interesting insight from external research about this passage is that it warns against the folly of idolatry and the worship of man-made gods. It emphasizes the absurdity of crafting idols from materials that are also used for practical purposes, such as fueling fire or making tools. The passage highlights the irony of people turning to these powerless idols for salvation and guidance, instead of placing their trust in the one true God.
Behindinsights3: An interesting insight about this passage is the reference to Melchizedek in verse 20. Melchizedek was a priest-king mentioned in the Old Testament, who was seen as a type or foreshadowing of Jesus Christ. This passage highlights Jesus' role as a high priest in the order of Melchizedek, emphasizing his eternal priesthood and his ability to intercede on behalf of believers.
SocioSummary1: The socio-historical context of the passage is not explicitly mentioned, but it is believed to have been written during a time of external threats and opposition to Israel. The psalmist describes arrogant and ruthless enemies who seek to kill him, indicating a period of conflict and danger. The Book of Psalms covers various historical periods, making it difficult to pinpoint an exact time for this psalm. However, the themes of seeking God's guidance, deliverance from enemies, and praising His faithfulness can apply to different socio-historical contexts in Israel's history.
SocioSummary2: The passage in question was written in the ancient Near East during the time of Isaiah, around the 8th century BCE. It is set in a socio-political context where the worship of idols was common. Isaiah criticizes this practice, asserting that idols hold no value and those who create and worship them will be embarrassed and afraid. The passage highlights the clash between monotheism and idol worship, with Isaiah advocating for the former and condemning the latter.
SocioSummary3: The passage is from the book of Hebrews in the New Testament, believed to have been written during the reign of Emperor Nero in the Roman Empire. The Roman Empire was in a period of stability known as Pax Romana, enforcing Roman law and governance across Europe, North Africa, and the Middle East. In the Middle East, the Jewish people lived under Roman rule and faced tensions and conflicts with the occupying forces. The audience of Hebrews was likely Jewish Christians facing persecution and pressure to abandon their faith in Jesus.

Scene1: A scene from the movie "The Lion King" is similar to this passage. In this scene, Simba, feeling lost and abandoned, prays for guidance and asks to be taught the ways of his father, the king. He also expresses his fear and praises the greatness of his father. Similarly, in the passage, the speaker asks God for guidance, expresses their fear, and praises God's love and faithfulness. Both the scene and passage depict a plea for help and an acknowledgment of God's compassion and strength.
Scene2: The scene from the movie that is similar to this passage is from the film American Psycho (2000) where the main character, Patrick Bateman, meticulously goes through his morning routine, focusing on his elaborate skincare and grooming rituals. The passage describes the absurdity and futility of idolizing material objects and the false sense of power and satisfaction it provides.
Scene3: A scene from the movie "The Matrix" is similar to this passage. In the scene where Neo meets the Oracle, she assures him that he is "The One" and confirms it by telling him specific details about his future. This scene mirrors the idea of God's promise to Abraham, where God confirms his promise with an oath and reassures Abraham of his blessings and descendants.

emily1:
Teach me your way, O Lord divine,
Lend me an undivided heart,
That I, with faithfulness, may find
The path where fear and hope depart.

Praise you, my God, with all my might,
Glorify your name eternally,
For your love, from the depths of night,
Saved me and set my spirit free.

Arrogant foes besiege my soul,
With callous hearts, they seek my end,
Yet your mercy, Lord, makes me whole,
Your love and grace they cannot fend.

Turn to me, with your strength embrace,
Show mercy on your humble one,
For I, your servant in this space,
Serve you as my dear mother's son.

Give me a sign of your goodness, Lord,
That my enemies may see and sway,
Shamed by the help you did afford,
And comfort you bestowed each day.

Psalm 86:11-17, inspired by the style of Emily Dickinson, reminds us to seek God's guidance, yearning for an undivided heart and a reverence for His name. It reminds us to praise Him wholeheartedly for His boundless love and deliverance from darkness. And in the face of adversity, it showcases God's compassion, urging us to call upon His strength and mercy. Ultimately, the scripture encourages us to trust in His goodness and find comfort in His presence, even amidst trials and enemies.
emily2:
In the realm of crafted gods, we find
The treasures of a foolish mind,
Idols formed with human art,
But empty vessels in the heart.

The blacksmith toils with fiery might,
Forging idols for the night,
Yet hunger gnaws, his strength it takes,
And water's thirst he never slakes.

The carpenter with careful skill,
Measures, marks, and shapes at will,
Creating gods in human guise,
To dwell within his fragile lies.

From cedars cut, from forests vast,
He seeks his gods, they shall not last,
For fuel they burn, to warm his soul,
But worship them, he seeks control.

Half the wood goes to feed the flame,
The other to seek praise and fame,
He bows, he prays, he pleads for grace,
In his idol's false embrace.

Oh, how we too, with blinded gaze,
Can fashion gods in countless ways,
In pursuit of things we hold so dear,
We lose ourselves and drown in fear.

For idols built do not sustain,
They only mask our inner pain,
Let us turn from their empty guise,
And seek the love that truly satisfies.
emily3:
In promises divine, we find our peace,
When oaths from heaven declare release.
No greater witness could we desire,
Than God Himself, our souls inspire.

Abraham, patient, waited long,
He trusted in God's promise strong.
And so, in time, the blessing came,
His descendants vast, beyond all name.

The unchanging nature of His will,
He sealed with an oath, to fulfill.
Through two unchangeable things combined,
God's truth and faithfulness entwined.

We, who in hope have taken flight,
Find refuge in His holy light.
Our anchor secure, within His grace,
In the sanctuary, a hallowed place.

Our forerunner, Jesus, leads the way,
A high priest, forever, we obey.
He intercedes for us, day and night,
In the order of Melchizedek's might.

Oh, let this scripture touch our core,
And make us ponder more and more.
In God's promises, we find release,
Our souls they anchor, in perfect peace.
Snoop1:
Yo, Lord, teach me how to roll,
So I can trust in your loyalty, take control,
Give me a heart, undivided and true,
A heart that's filled, with reverence for you.

I'ma praise you, my God, with all my soul,
Forever I'll lift your name, reach the highest goal,
Your love for me is incredible, no doubt,
From the depths, you rescued me, pulled me out.

These haters be coming, trying to take my life,
But they don't care 'bout you, they living in strife,
But you, Lord, you be compassionate and kind,
Slow to anger, love and faithfulness combined.

Turn to me, show mercy, and display your might,
Save me, I'm your servant, serving day and night,
Give me a sign of your goodness, make it clear,
So my enemies see it, and feel the fear.

You've helped me, comforted me, every single day,
So I'll keep praising you, never gonna stray,
Lord, I'm grateful, you're always by my side,
In your love, I'll forever reside.
Snoop2:
Yo, listen up, I got a story to tell,
'Bout some fools who worship idols, it ain't goin' well,
They think these things are priceless, but they ain't worth a dime,
Blinded by their ignorance, it's a shame, such a crime.

They try to shape they gods, like they got some power,
But these craftsmen just humans, ain't nothin' to devour,
They gather 'round, thinkin' they stand tall,
But they'll be brought down low, terror and shame gonna fall.

The blacksmith in the forge, workin' with his tools,
Forgin' idols with hammers, thinkin' he makin' rules,
But hunger strikes, his strength fades away,
He thirsty, parched, his energy astray.

Then comes the carpenter, with his chisels and lines,
Drawin' out his idol, thinkin' it shines,
He shapes it like a human, all glorious and grand,
Hopin' it'll find a place in a worshipper's land.

Cutting down them cedars, or a cypress or oak,
Plantin' 'em in the forest, watchin' 'em soak,
Some fuel for the fire, some warmth for their soul,
But they take the leftover, their idol's role.

Half the wood goes in the fire, cookin' their meals,
Roastin' the meat, satisfyin' their feels,
Warm by the fire, sayin' "I'm feelin' right,
I see the flame, baby, it's lightin' up the night."

But the other half, it becomes their god,
Bowin' down, worshipin' like they ain't got a facade,
They pray for salvation, sayin', "You my king,
Save me, my god, you make my heart sing."

Now you heard the tale, 'bout them foolish guys,
Makin' idols, unaware of the lies,
Remember, my homies, don't fall into the trap,
Serve the true God, don't get caught in that crap.
Snoop3:
Yo, listen up, I got a story to tell,
'Bout Abraham, a playa, he did it well.
When God made a promise, there was no one higher,
Swore by himself, that's some next-level fire.

He said, "Abraham, you gon' be blessed,
With descendants aplenty, better than the rest."
So my man waited, he had patience in his soul,
And finally, God delivered, made him whole.

Now, people swear by others to seal the deal,
But God's oath, it's the truth, nothin' to conceal.
He wanted to show, his purpose won't change,
So he confirmed it with an oath, no room for exchange.

God don't lie, it's impossible for him,
So take hold of the hope, let your light start to dim.
We got this anchor for the soul, steady and tight,
In the inner sanctuary, where darkness takes flight.

Jesus, our forerunner, went behind the curtain,
Entered on our behalf, no need to be hurtin'.
He's the forever high priest, in Melchizedek's clan,
Hold on to that promise, let it guide your life's plan.

So remember, when life gets tough and you feel stuck,
God's promise is real, like a platinum-plated truck.
Have faith, my homies, keep your hope locked in,
God's got your back, from beginning to the end.
moviesummary1: This passage explores the themes of faith, redemption, and the triumph of good over evil. It highlights the importance of seeking God's guidance and protection in times of trial. Similar themes can be found in works such as The Lord of the Rings, The Chronicles of Narnia, Les Misérables, The Shawshank Redemption, and The Book of Eli. However, these works are not directly related to the passage.
moviesummary2: This passage from Isaiah 44:9-17 addresses the foolishness of idol worship and the futility of relying on man-made objects for guidance and satisfaction. It highlights the irony of crafting a physical object and then bowing down to worship it, emphasizing the absurdity of idolatry and false beliefs. The passage serves as a warning about the consequences of placing too much value on material possessions and the dangers of idolizing objects rather than focusing on a higher power. This theme of idolatry and false worship can be found in various works of literature and film that explore the allure and destructive power of idolizing objects, pursuing material wealth, and the consequences of false beliefs.
moviesummary3: The passage from Hebrews 6:13-20 shares similar themes and storylines with various novels and movies, including The Promise, The Ten Commandments, Life of Pi, The Prince of Egypt, and The Alchemist. These works explore themes of faith, promises, waiting, hope, and the perseverance of the human spirit.

Christiansong1: One Christian song that reflects the sentiments and situations described in Psalm 86:11-17 is "He Will Carry You" by Scott Wesley Brown. This song emphasizes the trust and reliance on God, acknowledging His faithfulness, love, and mercy even in the midst of challenges and attacks from enemies. It also highlights the comfort and deliverance found in serving and following God.
Cristiansong2:
A Christian song that describes the sentiment or situation in this passage is "You Are My God" by Jonathan Stockstill. The lyrics of this song express the foolishness of idol worship and emphasize the greatness and power of God:

You are my God, there's no other like You
You are my God, Your ways are higher
You are my God, Your love is unchanging
You are my God, forever and always

In the passage, Isaiah speaks against those who craft idols and worship them, highlighting their foolishness and the worthlessness of their idols. The song echoes this sentiment by declaring that there is no other like God and emphasizing His supremacy.
Christiansong3: One Christian song that describes the sentiment of Hebrews 6:13-20 is "Cornerstone" by Hillsong Worship. This song speaks about Jesus being our firm foundation and anchor for our souls, just as Hebrews 6:19 describes our hope as an anchor. The lyrics emphasize the unchanging nature of God's promises and our trust in Jesus as our forerunner and high priest forever.

breathprayer1:

Exhale: Grant an undivided heart today.
breathprayer2:
Inhale: God, all idols are worthless treasures.
Exhale: Save me, for You are God.
breathprayer3:
Inhale: Unchanging God, firm and secure.
Exhale: Anchor our souls in hope.
applicationq1: How can we seek God's guidance, rely on His faithfulness, and cultivate an undivided heart that fears His name, amidst difficult circumstances and attacks from adversaries?
applicationq2: What are some examples of idols that people may treasure in their lives, and how can we guard against putting our trust in them?
applicationq3: How can the unchanging nature of God's promise and His faithfulness encourage us to have hope and anchor our souls in Him?
Enneagram1:
Type 2 (The Helper):

- May resonate with verses 11-12, as they value guidance and dependence on a higher power.
- Would appreciate the mention of God's love and deliverance in verse 13, finding comfort and reassurance in it.
- Might feel particularly upset by the presence of arrogant and ruthless foes in verse 14.
- Would find solace in the descriptions of God's compassion and grace in verse 15.
- Would pray for God's mercy, strength, and salvation in verse 16, emphasizing their dedication to serving God.
- Would desire a sign of God's goodness to humiliate their enemies in verse 17.

Type 6 (The Loyalist):

- Would likely relate to the plea for guidance and an undivided heart in verse 11, seeking assurance and security.
- May struggle with excessive worry in the face of arrogant and ruthless foes mentioned in verse 14.
- Would find comfort in the description of God's compassionate and gracious nature in verse 15, relying on this attribute for support.
- Would request God's mercy and strength for protection in verse 16, emphasizing their loyalty to God's service.
- Would seek a sign of God's goodness to demonstrate His support and bring comfort in verse 17.
Enneagram2:

- Feels a sense of righteousness and indignation towards those who make idols.
- Employs critical thinking to analyze the consequences of idol worship.
- Strives to maintain integrity by avoiding any form of idolatry.
- May feel the need to educate others about the futility of idol worship.
- Evaluates their own behavior to ensure they are not valuing material possessions or false idols.

Type 7 (Enthusiast) may respond to this passage:

- Finds the passage intriguing and thought-provoking.
- May see it as an opportunity for intellectual exploration and debate.
- Recognizes the dangers of idolatry, but also finds fascination in the intricacy of idol-making.
- Appreciates the vivid imagery and storytelling in the passage.
- May struggle with the idea of restriction and refraining from worshipping idols due to their desire for new experiences and interests.

- Engages with the passage by analyzing and interpreting the logical order and structure of the argument presented.
- Appreciates the clarity and certainty conveyed in the passage.
- Might find encouragement in the idea of an unchanging nature and purpose of God.
- Focuses on the importance of remaining steadfast in hope and finding security in faith.

Type 7 (The Enthusiast):

- Finds excitement in the promise of God's blessings and abundant descendants to Abraham.
- Identifies with the concept of waiting patiently and being rewarded.
- Views the passage as a source of hope and inspiration, particularly in relation to the promise of a firm and secure anchor for the soul.

Myersbriggs3:

- An ENFP might find inspiration in the passage, resonating with the theme of hope and finding comfort in the idea of an anchor for the soul. They might also be drawn to the mention of Jesus as a high priest, seeing it as a symbol of compassion and advocacy.
psychology1:
One theme from this text is the reliance on God's guidance and the desire for an undivided heart to fear His name (verse 11). This can be connected to findings in contemporary psychology research that highlight the importance of having a sense of purpose and a strong moral compass, which are associated with increased well-being and resilience (e.g., Damon & Colby, 2021; Maddi, 2013). 

Citations:
- Damon, W., & Colby, A. (2021). The power of purpose. Oxford University Press.
- Maddi, S. R. (2013). Resilience at work: How to succeed no matter what life throws at you. AMACOM.
psychology2: One theme from this text is the folly of idol worship and the emptiness of placing value in material possessions. Contemporary psychology research has found that materialism and the pursuit of wealth and possessions have negative effects on well-being and happiness. Studies show that prioritizing material goods can lead to lower life satisfaction, increased stress, and a diminished sense of meaning and purpose in life. The research highlights the importance of focusing on relationships and experiences rather than on accumulating material wealth. (Sources: Kasser, T., & Kasser, V. (2001). The dreams of people high and low in materialism. Journal of Economic Psychology, 22(6), 693-719. Dittmar, H. (2008). Consumer culture, identity, and well-being: The search for the "good life" and the "body perfect". Psychology & Marketing, 25(6), 566-586.)
psychology3: One theme from this text is the certainty of God's promise. In contemporary psychology research, this theme can be related to the concept of hope and its effects on mental health. Research has shown that having hope can improve psychological well-being, resilience, and the ability to cope with challenges. It serves as an anchor for the soul, providing a sense of security and encouragement. (Sources: Snyder, C. R., Cheavens, J., & Sympson, S. C. (1997). Hope: An individual motive for social commerce. Group Dynamics: Theory, Research, and Practice, 1(2), 107-118. Marques, S. C., Lopez, S. J., & Pais-Ribeiro, J. L. (2011). "Building Hope for the Future": A Program to Foster Strengths in Middle-School Students. The Journal of Happiness & Well-Being, 3(1), 135-150.)

biology3:
The theme of certainty and the unchanging nature of God's promise can be applied to the scientific finding of the conservation of genetic information. The DNA sequence, which contains the genetic information of an organism, remains stable and unchanged across generations. This stability ensures the continuity of traits and the passing down of inherited characteristics. Just as God's promise to Abraham was unchangeable, the genetic information stored in DNA remains constant, providing certainty in the transmission of traits through inheritance.

Research Sources:
- Watson JD, Crick FH. Molecular structure of nucleic acids; a structure for deoxyribose nucleic acid. Nature. 1953 Apr 25;171(4356):737-8. doi: 10.1038/171737a0. PMID: 13054692.
- Alberts B, Johnson A, Lewis J, et al. Molecular Biology of the Cell. 4th edition. New York: Garland Science; 2002. Section 2.1, DNA Is a Double Helix Joined by Complementary Base Pairing. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK26871/
Haiku 1: Teach me, Lord, your way, with undivided heart, I'll fear and praise your name.

Haiku 3: God's promise to all   unchanging, firm and certain   Hope, our anchor strong
